case_name: DDVP control in river water for drinking supply under strict effluent, low-energy, and chemical-minimization constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for DDVP-contaminated river water intended for drinking water production while balancing strict finished-water limits, low-energy preference, and minimization of chemical consumption.

input_data.scenario_background: A mid-size surface water plant draws from a river downstream of mixed agricultural and peri-urban land uses, where episodic pesticide runoff has led to detectable DDVP in the intake during storm and irrigation return-flow periods. The utility must achieve very low finished-water concentrations suitable for potable use, but it is under pressure to avoid energy-intensive treatment and to minimize continuous oxidant or specialty chemical consumption. Conventional clarification is already familiar to operators, so the key trade-off is whether to rely mainly on adsorption, use a stronger oxidation barrier with added chemicals, or install membrane treatment that is technically effective but operationally misaligned.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve stable potable compliance with very low pesticide concentrations, including robust control of DDVP under variable surface-water conditions.
secondary_goals: minimize continuous chemical addition beyond standard coagulation and disinfection
maintain manageable operator workload during seasonal contaminant spikes

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer conventional solids residuals and avoid difficult concentrate streams or high-frequency specialty media replacement
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: must tolerate seasonal raw-water turbidity and pesticide variability without frequent process upset

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids and reduce natural organic matter loading to downstream adsorption
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_filtration: polish clarified water and protect GAC from excess particulate fouling
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb DDVP and co-occurring trace organic pesticides while providing an additional barrier for taste and odor organics
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final pathogen inactivation for potable supply

applicability_rationale: surface-water pretreatment reduces turbidity and some DOC, improving downstream carbon performance for micropollutant adsorption
GAC is a widely used low-energy barrier for pesticides in drinking water treatment and avoids high-pressure membrane energy demand
this route fits the utility preference to minimize specialty chemical addition because the main advanced barrier is adsorptive rather than oxidation-intensive
the process chain remains familiar to conventional plant operators and is easier to integrate into an existing river-water facility than ozone-biological systems or RO
although DOC is moderate and will consume adsorption capacity, the combination of clarification plus GAC offers a practical balance of compliance reliability and manageable O&M
potential_risks: higher DOC and seasonal runoff events can shorten GAC bed life and require careful breakthrough monitoring
spent carbon replacement or reactivation logistics still need planning
if influent DDVP spikes become frequent and severe, single-stage GAC may need EBCT optimization or lead-lag operation

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove bulk particulates and support downstream micropollutant control
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_filtration: provide conventional filtration prior to or integrated with PAC application strategy
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_contact: provide flexible adsorptive capture of DDVP during episodic contamination events
core_unit_functions.granular_media_filtration: remove PAC-associated solids carryover and polish treated water

applicability_rationale: PAC is technically suitable for episodic pesticide events and can be dose-adjusted during seasonal DDVP peaks
this approach preserves low energy demand and can be attractive where contaminant occurrence is intermittent rather than constant
compared with fixed-bed GAC, PAC may reduce long idle media inventory and offer operational flexibility for a variable river source
the route uses adsorption rather than advanced oxidation, helping align with chemical-minimization goals better than ozone-based options when applied only during events
potential_risks: performance depends strongly on contact time, mixing, dose control, and competition from DOC
continuous or repeated PAC use increases sludge production and solids handling burden
less robust than fixed-bed GAC for maintaining a constant polishing barrier under sudden spikes if feed control is delayed

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce particulate and organic loading before oxidation
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_filtration: provide pretreatment and lower fouling risk for biological polishing
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize DDVP and other susceptible trace organic contaminants
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_filtration: remove biodegradable oxidation byproducts and provide additional polishing

applicability_rationale: ozone can provide a strong treatment barrier for many pesticides and may reduce reliance on activated carbon replacement
biological filtration helps manage oxidation transformation products and can improve overall finished-water stability
this route can be attractive when the utility values a more aggressive contaminant destruction step rather than transfer to spent media
potential_risks: ozone generation increases energy demand and process complexity relative to adsorption-based options
oxidation may form byproducts or transformation products that require verification and downstream biological polishing
bromate control must be assessed if bromide is present even at modest levels
chemical-minimization preference is weaker for this route because it introduces an advanced oxidant barrier

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove solids and reduce membrane fouling load
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_filtration: provide pretreatment before pressure membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: reject dissolved organic micropollutants including DDVP and some co-occurring contaminants
core_unit_functions.post_stabilization: adjust finished-water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: nanofiltration is technically capable of providing a strong physical barrier for many low-level organic contaminants
this option may be favored if future source-water deterioration is expected and broader contaminant control is desired beyond current pesticides
potential_risks: concentrate management is difficult for a pesticide-bearing waste stream and conflicts with the residuals constraint
pressure membranes require higher energy and more specialized O&M than preferred for this utility
pretreatment, fouling control, and post-treatment stabilization add complexity compared with adsorption-based routes

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended matter before advanced treatment
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_filtration: polish clarified water and improve UV transmittance relative to raw water
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_uv_hydrogen_peroxide: oxidize DDVP through hydroxyl radical chemistry

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation is included as a technically capable but less aligned option where destructive treatment is prioritized
it may be considered if pilot testing demonstrates unusually favorable DDVP degradation and if the utility later accepts higher operational sophistication
potential_risks: UV-AOP has substantial energy demand and requires ongoing hydrogen peroxide dosing, directly conflicting with low-energy and chemical-minimization priorities
natural organic matter can scavenge radicals and reduce treatment efficiency in river water
process control and verification of byproducts or incomplete transformation increase monitoring needs

preferred_rank_pattern: adsorption-based routes should rank near the top because they balance DDVP removal with low energy use
flexible PAC should appear as a plausible alternative for episodic river-water contamination but not necessarily above fixed-bed GAC
oxidation and membrane routes should rank lower when strict effluent goals must be balanced against chemical minimization and energy sensitivity
at least one technically capable option should be downgraded mainly because of concentrate handling or operational mismatch

must_reflect: strict finished-water DDVP control is necessary for potable compliance
low-energy preference disfavors high-pressure membranes and energy-intensive AOPs
chemical minimization disfavors treatment trains relying on continuous advanced oxidant dosing
surface-water variability requires a robust barrier that can tolerate turbidity and DOC changes

must_contain: why conventional clarification ahead of polishing matters for river water with moderate turbidity and DOC
why adsorption is attractive for pesticide removal under low-energy and chemical-minimization constraints
why moderate DOC can reduce adsorption capacity and must be acknowledged
why ozone or UV-AOP are technically plausible yet penalized by energy and process-complexity considerations
why membrane options are downgraded due to concentrate management and operational burden

should_mention: GAC or PAC breakthrough and capacity loss due to DOC competition
oxidation byproduct or transformation product uncertainty
need for pilot testing or monitoring under variable river conditions
